pages.raw_text:
Following in the wake of the
publication of the story that the
Northern Pacific and Great North
ern are going to join hands in the
construction of a railroad along the
north shore of the Columbia river
from Pasco to Vancouver, has come
a revival of the myth that it is i'm
possible to construct such a road
because the o. R. & n. interests
control two trategic points on the
proposed route.


The ancient legend is to the eff
feet that when Henry Willard was
projecting and constructing the O..
r. & n. railroad he looked about
to cut off the possibility of having
his line paralelled by a competing
road on the opposite shore of the
Columbia. The story is projected
into more recent history by the
